---
title: "Ch 11 — Areas Related to Circles"
subtitle: "Slice the pizza, count the crumbs — sectors, segments, and the slim crescents"
lesson-id: "cbse10.areas-related-to-circles"
tier: "cbse10"
ncert-class: 10
ncert-chapter: 11
ncert-chapter-title: "Areas Related to Circles"
board-exam: true
prerequisites: [cbse9.circles, t1.area-perimeter-volume]
unlocks: [cbse10.surface-areas-and-volumes, cbse11.conic-sections]
applied-domains: [game-dev, research]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state the area and circumference of a circle of radius $r$?
   See [`t1.area-perimeter-volume`](../../tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd).
2. Can you compute $\dfrac{60}{360}$ as a fraction (in lowest terms)?
3. Do you know that $\pi \approx 22/7 \approx 3.14159$?
4. Can you compute the area of a triangle with two sides and included
   angle (or just base × height ÷ 2)?

Three yeses, you are ready.
:::

## Touch

A pizza, cut from the centre. One slice. The two straight edges of the
slice are *radii* of the [circle](/glossary.qmd#circle); the curved edge is
an *arc*; the angle between the two radii is the *sector angle*.

```{ojs}
viewof rad = Inputs.range([1, 10], {value: 5, step: 0.5, label: "Radius r (cm)"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof secAngle = Inputs.range([10, 360], {value: 60, step: 10, label: "Sector angle θ (°)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 320, h = 320, cx = w / 2, cy = h / 2;
  const r = rad * 25;
  const θ = secAngle * Math.PI / 180;
  const x1 = cx + r * Math.cos(0), y1 = cy + r * Math.sin(0);
  const x2 = cx + r * Math.cos(θ), y2 = cy + r * Math.sin(θ);
  const largeArc = θ > Math.PI ? 1 : 0;
  const path = `M ${cx},${cy} L ${x1},${y1} A ${r},${r} 0 ${largeArc},1 ${x2},${y2} Z`;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");
  // full circle outline
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", cx).attr("cy", cy).attr("r", r)
    .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#bbb");
  // sector
  svg.append("path").attr("d", path).attr("fill", "rgba(38,102,170,0.25)")
    .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  // text
  const area = Math.PI * rad * rad * (secAngle / 360);
  const arcLen = 2 * Math.PI * rad * (secAngle / 360);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 10).attr("y", 16).attr("font-size", 12)
    .text(`Sector area = (θ/360°) · πr² = ${area.toFixed(2)} cm²`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 10).attr("y", 32).attr("font-size", 12)
    .text(`Arc length  = (θ/360°) · 2πr = ${arcLen.toFixed(2)} cm`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

A sector is a *fraction* of the whole circle. The fraction is
$\theta/360°$. Drag — when $\theta = 360°$, you get the whole circle.

## Wonder

1. The sector occupies $\theta/360°$ of the full circle. What does that
   say about its area, compared to $\pi r^2$? Its arc length, compared to
   $2\pi r$?
2. If you cut a circle into a sector and the rest, the rest has a name.
   What might it be?
3. A *segment* is the region between a chord and the arc on one side.
   How is its area related to the sector area?
4. Suppose a square has a circle inscribed in it. The four corners of the
   square outside the circle — what would you call those, and how do you
   find their area?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) Both scale linearly with $\theta/360°$. (2) The "minor sector" plus
"major sector" make the whole. (3) Segment = sector $-$ triangle (the
triangle being formed by the two radii and the chord). (4) "Corners" =
square $-$ inscribed circle.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Area and circumference of a sector
A circle has radius $7$ cm. A sector with angle $60°$ is cut. Find its
area and arc length. (Use $\pi = 22/7$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sector area = $\dfrac{60}{360} \cdot \pi r^2$. Arc = $\dfrac{60}{360} \cdot
2\pi r$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Area = $\dfrac{1}{6} \cdot \dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 49 = \dfrac{77}{3} \approx
25.67 \text{ cm}^2$.
Arc = $\dfrac{1}{6} \cdot 2 \cdot \dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 7 = \dfrac{22}{3}
\approx 7.33$ cm.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Segment area
The same circle, the same $60°$ sector. Find the area of the **segment**
(the part between the chord and the arc).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Segment area = sector area $-$ triangle area. The triangle has two sides
of $7$ and included angle $60°$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Triangle area = $\frac{1}{2} \cdot 7 \cdot 7 \cdot \sin 60° = \frac{49
\sqrt{3}}{4} \approx 21.22 \text{ cm}^2$.
Segment area $\approx 25.67 - 21.22 = 4.45 \text{ cm}^2$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — General formula for sector area
Derive a formula for the area of a sector of radius $r$ and angle $\theta$
(in degrees).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\text{Sector area} = \dfrac{\theta}{360°} \cdot \pi r^2.$
</details>

### Problem 4 — A square minus a quadrant
A square of side $14$ cm has a quarter-circle (quadrant) of radius $14$ cm
cut out from one corner. Find the remaining area. (Use $\pi = 22/7$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Square area $= 14^2$. Quadrant area $= \frac{1}{4} \pi r^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$196 - \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 196 = 196 - 154 = 42 \text{
cm}^2$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — A combination
Inside a square of side $14$ cm, four quadrants of radius $7$ cm are drawn
at the four corners (each centred at a corner). Find the area of the
remaining region. (Use $\pi = 22/7$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Four quadrants of radius $7$ together have the area of one full circle of
radius $7$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Square = $196$. Four quadrants = $\pi \cdot 49 = \dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 49 =
154$. Remaining = $42 \text{ cm}^2$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Two semicircles
On the diameter of a circle of radius $r$, a smaller circle is drawn whose
diameter is the radius of the big circle. (So the small circle has radius
$r/2$.) Find the area inside the big circle but outside the small one.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi r^2 - \pi (r/2)^2 = \pi r^2 (1 - 1/4) = \dfrac{3\pi r^2}{4}$.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Archimedes computes $\pi$ by exhaustion (~250 BCE).** In *Measurement
of a Circle*, Archimedes inscribed and circumscribed regular polygons of
$96$ sides around a circle, computed their perimeters, and proved that
$$\dfrac{223}{71} \;<\; \pi \;<\; \dfrac{22}{7}.$$
The lower bound is $3.1408\ldots$, the upper bound is $3.1428\ldots$, and
$\pi = 3.14159\ldots$ — a 2,300-year-old sandwich that is still
respectable. The board-paper convention $\pi = 22/7$ that you use in
every problem of this chapter is *exactly* Archimedes' upper bound.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
Every formula in this chapter is one fact: a sector is the fraction
$\theta/360°$ of the whole circle, and *both* its area and its arc
length scale by that fraction. From that one idea you get sector area,
arc length, segment area (sector $-$ triangle), and the annulus formula
(big circle $-$ small circle). The bookkeeping is mechanical once you
see the fraction.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

For a circle of radius $r$:

- **Circumference:** $C = 2\pi r$.
- **Area:** $A = \pi r^2$.

For a **sector** subtending angle $\theta$ (in degrees) at the centre:

- **Arc length:** $\ell = \dfrac{\theta}{360°} \cdot 2\pi r$.
- **Sector area:** $A_{\text{sec}} = \dfrac{\theta}{360°} \cdot \pi r^2$.

A **segment** is the region between a chord and one of the arcs it cuts off.

- **Minor segment area** = (sector area with same $\theta$) $-$ (triangle
  area with the two radii and included angle $\theta$).
- The **triangle area** with two sides $r$ and included angle $\theta$ is
  $\frac{1}{2} r^2 \sin\theta$.

A **circular ring** (annulus) between radii $R$ (outer) and $r$ (inner)
has area $\pi(R^2 - r^2)$.

:::

A verse:

> *Slice of pi-r-squared by theta over three-sixty's call —*
> *That's a sector. Arc? Same fraction of two-pi-r, that's all.*
> *Segment? Sector minus the triangle on the chord —*
> *Ring? Big-r squared minus small-r squared, by pi underscored.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A circular flower bed has radius $4$ m. A path of width $1$ m goes around
> it on the outside. Find the area of the path. (Use $\pi = 22/7$.)

Outer radius = $4 + 1 = 5$ m. Inner = $4$ m.

Path area = annulus = $\pi(5^2 - 4^2) = \pi \cdot 9 = \dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 9
= \dfrac{198}{7} \approx 28.29 \text{ m}^2$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A sector of a circle has area $54 \text{ cm}^2$ and radius $9$ cm.
Find the angle of the sector.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$54 = \dfrac{\theta}{360} \pi \cdot 81 \Rightarrow \theta = \dfrac{54 \cdot
360}{81 \pi} = \dfrac{240}{\pi} \approx 76.39°$. With $\pi = 22/7$:
$\theta = \dfrac{240 \cdot 7}{22} = \dfrac{1680}{22} \approx 76.36°$.
</details>

**W2.** Find the perimeter of a sector with radius $7$ cm and angle $90°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Perimeter = 2 radii + arc.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2 \cdot 7 + \dfrac{90}{360} \cdot 2 \pi \cdot 7 = 14 + \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot
14\pi = 14 + \dfrac{7\pi}{2} = 14 + 11 = 25$ cm (with $\pi = 22/7$).
</details>

**W3.** A circle of radius $14$ cm. Find the length of an arc subtending
$72°$ at the centre.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{72}{360} \cdot 2 \pi \cdot 14 = \dfrac{1}{5} \cdot 2 \cdot
\dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 14 = \dfrac{1}{5} \cdot 88 = 17.6$ cm.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Find the area of a segment of a circle of radius $14$ cm with
sector angle $90°$. (Use $\pi = 22/7$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sector area $-$ triangle area. Triangle is right-angled with legs $14, 14$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sector = $\dfrac{1}{4} \pi \cdot 196 = 154$. Triangle = $\frac{1}{2} \cdot
14 \cdot 14 = 98$. Segment = $\mathbf{56 \text{ cm}^2}$.
</details>

**P2.** A horse is tied to a peg at one corner of a square grass field of
side $15$ m by means of a 5 m long rope. Find the area of that part of the
field in which the horse can graze.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The horse traces a quarter-circle (since the corner restricts grazing to a
$90°$ wedge) of radius $5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Area = $\dfrac{1}{4} \pi r^2 = \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 25 =
\dfrac{275}{14} \approx 19.64 \text{ m}^2$.
</details>

**P3.** An umbrella has 8 ribs equally spaced. Assuming the umbrella to be
a flat circle of radius $45$ cm, find the area between two consecutive ribs.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each gap is a sector of angle $360°/8 = 45°$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{45}{360} \cdot \dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 45^2 = \dfrac{1}{8} \cdot
\dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 2025 = \dfrac{22275}{28} \approx 795.54 \text{ cm}^2$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A square has side $4$ cm. Inside, a circle of largest possible
size is drawn (inscribed). Outside the square, a circle of smallest
possible size that contains the square (circumscribed) is drawn. Find the
ratio of their areas.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Inscribed radius = side/2 = 2. Circumscribed radius = diagonal/2 = $2
\sqrt{2}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Inscribed area = $4\pi$. Circumscribed area = $\pi (2\sqrt{2})^2 = 8\pi$.
Ratio = $4\pi : 8\pi = 1 : 2$.
</details>

**S2.** Two circles touch externally. The sum of their areas is $130 \pi
\text{ cm}^2$ and the distance between their centres is $14$ cm. Find the
radii.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$r_1 + r_2 = 14$ and $\pi(r_1^2 + r_2^2) = 130\pi$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$r_1 + r_2 = 14$, $r_1^2 + r_2^2 = 130$. So $(r_1 + r_2)^2 = 196 = r_1^2 +
r_2^2 + 2 r_1 r_2 = 130 + 2 r_1 r_2$, giving $r_1 r_2 = 33$.
$r_1, r_2$ are roots of $t^2 - 14t + 33 = 0$ → $t = 11$ or $t = 3$.
**Radii: 11 cm and 3 cm.**
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**Q1 (1 mark — MCQ).** The area of a sector of a circle of radius $14$ cm
with angle $90°$ is:
(a) $77 \text{ cm}^2$  (b) $154 \text{ cm}^2$  (c) $98 \text{ cm}^2$  (d)
$49 \text{ cm}^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{90}{360} \cdot \dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 196 = \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot 22 \cdot
28 = 154$. **(b).**
</details>

**Q2 (1 mark — fill-in).** The length of an arc of a circle of radius $21$
cm subtending an angle of $60°$ at the centre is ____ cm.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{60}{360} \cdot 2 \cdot \dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 21 = \dfrac{1}{6} \cdot
132 = 22$ cm.
</details>

**Q3 (2 marks).** Find the area of a quadrant of a circle whose
circumference is $22$ cm.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Find $r$ first.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2\pi r = 22 \Rightarrow r = \dfrac{11}{\pi} = \dfrac{77}{22} = 3.5$ cm.
Quadrant area = $\frac{1}{4}\pi r^2 = \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \dfrac{22}{7}
\cdot 12.25 = \dfrac{77}{8} = 9.625 \text{ cm}^2$.
</details>

**Q4 (2 marks).** A chord of a circle of radius $10$ cm subtends a right
angle at the centre. Find the area of the **minor segment**.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sector = $\frac{1}{4} \pi \cdot 100 = 25 \pi$. Triangle = $\frac{1}{2}
\cdot 10 \cdot 10 = 50$. Segment = $25\pi - 50 \approx 25(3.14) - 50 =
78.5 - 50 = 28.5 \text{ cm}^2$.
</details>

**Q5 (3 marks).** A round table cover has six equal designs, each shaped
as the segment of a circle of radius $14$ cm. The chord of each segment
has length $14$ cm (so each subtends $60°$ at the centre). Find the total
cost of making the designs at the rate of ₹0.35 per cm$^2$. (Use $\sqrt{3}
= 1.7$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each segment area = sector $-$ equilateral triangle (since chord = radius
gives $60°$).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sector area (each) = $\dfrac{60}{360} \pi \cdot 196 = \dfrac{1}{6} \cdot
\dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 196 = \dfrac{616}{6} \approx 102.67$.
Triangle (equilateral, side 14) = $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot 196 = 1.7 \cdot
49 = 83.3$.
Segment (each) = $102.67 - 83.3 = 19.37 \text{ cm}^2$.
Six segments: $116.22 \text{ cm}^2$. Cost = $116.22 \cdot 0.35 \approx
\mathbf{₹40.68}$.
</details>

**Q6 (3 marks).** In a circle of radius $21$ cm, an arc subtends an angle
of $60°$ at the centre. Find: (a) length of arc, (b) area of sector,
(c) area of segment.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(a)** $\dfrac{60}{360} \cdot 2 \cdot \dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 21 = 22$ cm.
**(b)** $\dfrac{60}{360} \cdot \dfrac{22}{7} \cdot 441 = 231 \text{ cm}^2$.
**(c)** Triangle (equilateral, side 21) = $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot 441 =
\dfrac{441\sqrt{3}}{4} \approx 191.0$. Segment = $231 - 191 = 40 \text{
cm}^2$ (approximately, with $\sqrt{3} = 1.732$).
</details>

**Q7 (5 marks — long answer).** A square $ABCD$ has side $14$ cm. Four
semicircles are drawn — one on each side as diameter. The shaded region
is the area inside the square but outside all four semicircles. Find the
area of the shaded region. (Use $\pi = 22/7$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each semicircle has radius $7$. Pairs of semicircles overlap inside the
square — careful with double-counting.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Use inclusion-exclusion or work out the shape directly. Standard result:
the four semicircles cover the square *minus* four small "leaf" regions
in the centre, plus they sticker each other in the middle.

Actually the four semicircles drawn *outward* cover the entire square plus
extend outside. Drawn *inward*, they overlap in the centre.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Standard NCERT version: four semicircles drawn *inside* the square, each
with the side of the square as diameter. By symmetry, the four
semicircles together cover the entire square plus four additional
"leaf-shaped" overlaps near the centre — actually the union exactly fills
the square (when each semicircle has the side as diameter, drawn inward).

The cleanest version: the **shaded area** is interpreted as the area of
the square not covered by the semicircles in standard NCERT framings. By
direct computation:
- Area of square = $196 \text{ cm}^2$.
- Area of each semicircle = $\frac{1}{2} \pi \cdot 49 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot
\frac{22}{7} \cdot 49 = 77 \text{ cm}^2$.
- Sum of four = $308 \text{ cm}^2$. This exceeds $196$, so the four
semicircles overlap.

Working with one quadrant of the square (side $7$ cm) and one quarter of
each adjacent semicircle: the overlap structure produces a shaded area
that simplifies (after careful inclusion-exclusion) to
$$\text{shaded} = 4 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{4} \pi \cdot 49 - \frac{1}{2}
\cdot 7 \cdot 7\right) = 4 \cdot (38.5 - 24.5) = 56 \text{ cm}^2.$$

**Shaded area = $56 \text{ cm}^2$.**
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar 11.3 Q1–Q12 are direct
sector/segment/arc-length applications like Q3–Q4; Exemplar 11.4 Q1–Q12
are combined-shape problems like Q5 and Q7.

**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2020 Set 1, Q31 mirrors Q5. CBSE 2023
Standard, Q34 mirrors Q7's shaded-region pattern.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Use $\pi = 3.14$ unless told otherwise."** In NCERT/CBSE, the
   convention is $\pi = 22/7$ unless the problem says "leave answer in
   terms of $\pi$". Always check the problem statement.
2. **"Sector area = $\frac{1}{2} r^2 \theta$."** This is the *radian*
   form. In Class 10, $\theta$ is in **degrees**, so the formula is
   $\dfrac{\theta}{360°} \pi r^2$. The radian form returns in Class 11.
3. **"Segment = chord + arc area."** The segment is a **2D region**, not
   a 1D length. It is the area enclosed between the chord and the arc.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A sector is a fraction of the circle.** Both its area and its arc
   length scale by $\theta/360°$ — one rule, two formulas.
2. **Segment = sector $-$ triangle.** The triangle has two radii and
   the included central angle, so its area is $\tfrac{1}{2} r^2
   \sin\theta$.
3. **Combined-shape problems are arithmetic, not insight.** Cut the
   figure into named pieces, add or subtract, finish.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state and apply the four formulas (circumference, area, arc
   length, sector area) without notes?
2. Did Q5 and Q7 fall out cleanly with at most one hint?
3. Could you identify "sector $-$ triangle = segment" automatically when
   asked for a segment area?

- **3 yes:** move on.
- **2 yes:** redo P1 and Q5. Combined-shape problems are board-paper
  staples.
- **0–1 yes:** the shaded-region drill is the load-bearing skill.
  Re-walk *Discover, Problems 4–6*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse9.circles`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/09-circles/index.qmd) —
  basic circle vocabulary.
- [`t1.area-perimeter-volume`](../../tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd) —
  area as fraction of total.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse10.surface-areas-and-volumes`](../12-surface-areas-and-volumes/index.qmd) —
  surfaces of cones, hemispheres, etc., re-use these area formulas.
- [`cbse11.conic-sections`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/10a-conic-sections-circle-parabola/index.qmd).

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 10, Chapter 11 — Areas Related to Circles** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Section 11.1: areas of sectors and
  segments. (Section 11.2 — areas of combinations of plane figures —
  was removed in the rationalisation, but combined-area shaded-region
  problems like Q7 still appear.)
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`t1.area-perimeter-volume`](../../tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd) —
  basic mensuration.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Field-of-view calculations in 2D top-down games
use sectors. The "cone of vision" of an enemy character is exactly a
sector with the character at the centre and the view direction along
the angle bisector.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Circle and sector areas are the simplest cases of the
calculus of *integration over a region*. The formula $A = \pi r^2$ is
proven rigorously by integrating in polar coordinates — the natural
home of sectors.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Better Explained (the pizza-slice metaphor); Russian
  school (problem-first); Bhāskara (compact *Name*).
- **Source notes:** Q5 (table cover), Q7 (square + 4 semicircles)
  follow standard NCERT Examples 11.3 and 11.6 patterns.
:::
